title: Discovering Palazzo Vecchio: History and Famous Rooms
highlights: Palazzo Vecchio was built between 1299 and 1314 as the seat of the Florentine Republic. Designed by Arnolfo di Cambio, the palace was originally known as the Palazzo dei Priori, home to the city’s governing magistrates. Its fortress-like  ...    Over time, the building’s role evolved. When the Medici family rose to power in the 16th century, the palace became the ducal residence of Cosimo I de’ Medici. Although Cosimo later moved to Palazzo Pitti, Palazzo Vecchio retained its importance as the administrative

title: Palazzo Vecchio Historical Facts and Pictures | The History Hub
highlights: Vecchio (pronounced in Italian as “paˈlattso ˈvɛkkjo” meaning “Old Palace”), initially known as Palazzo della Signoria, is the civic center of the city of Florence in Italy. It is connected by an enclosed passageway, known as the Vasari Corridor with the Palazzo Pitti. The palace was called by several other names like Palazzo del Popolo, Palazzo  ...    palace. Later additions were made in the 15th century by architect Michelozzo Michelozzi. title: Palazzo Vecchio - Wikipedia
highlights: Originally called the Palazzo della Signoria, after the Signoria of Florence, the ruling body of the Republic of Florence, this building was also known by several other names: Palazzo del Popolo, Palazzo dei Priori, and Palazzo Ducale, in accordance with the varying use of the palace during its long history. The building acquired its current name when the Medici duke's residence was moved across the Arno River to the Palazzo Pitti.  ...    Duke Cosimo I de' Medici (later to become grand duke) moved his official seat from the Medici palazzo in via Larga to the Palazzo della Signoria in May 1540, signalling the security of Medici power in Florence. When Cosimo later removed to Palazzo Pitti, he officially renamed his former palace the Palazzo Vecchio, the "Old Palace
